title: RFC: Add `(linux || freebsd)` build requirements
body:
Currently, users opening the Podman codebase in an IDE are hit with hundreds of "undeclared symbol" errors and the like, because a lot of code, e.g. in `libpod`, only has a `linux || freebsd` implementation, but is called from code without such a restriction.

Add explicit platform conditions to such files. Then the IDEs are silent by default — but once such a file is open (at least when the IDE is backed by `gopls`), the language handler parses the build directive and switches to assuming such a platform.

This is mostly a dumb search&replace, guided by the reported errors. More accurate build directives are probably possible in various places, or some code could be moved across files to not require the build directive at all.

Reviewers: The first commit contains _only_ a single kind of change (and that can be verified e.g. using `sort -u`). The other two commits are much smaller and need a more careful review, especially the last one.

#### Checklist

Ensure you have completed the following checklist for your pull request to be reviewed:
<!-- Use [x] to mark as done, or click the checkbox after opening PR -->

- [ ] Certify you wrote the patch or otherwise have the right to pass it on as an open-source patch by signing all
commits. (`git commit -s`). (If needed, use `git commit -s --amend`).  The author email must match
the sign-off email address. See [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#sign-your-prs)
for more information.
- [ ] Referenced issues using `Fixes: #00000` in commit message (if applicable)
- [ ] [Tests](https://github.com/containers/podman/tree/main/test#readme) have been added/updated (or no tests are needed)
- [ ] [Documentation](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/README.md) has been updated (or no documentation changes are needed)
- [ ] All commits pass `make validatepr` (format/lint checks)
- [ ] [Release note](https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/guide/release-notes.md) entered in the section below (or `None` if no user-facing changes)

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
None
```

comments.body: [NON-BLOCKING] Packit jobs failed. @containers/packit-build please check. Everyone else, feel free to ignore.
[NON-BLOCKING] Packit jobs failed. @containers/packit-build please check. Everyone else, feel free to ignore.
I really wish there was a way of defining a macro for this we could use everywhere. How many times do we have `!remote && (linux || freebsd)` in a file?

I do have a vague concern that this will allow Libpod to be leaked into the build on Mac/Windows and nobody will notice (right now, it's a straight compile error), though we probably still don't compile; there are no `_other.go` files that define the APIs that are compile-tagged out, after all.

> I really wish there was a way of defining a macro for this we could use everywhere. 

I don’t think that would help here – we could define a `native_platform` build tag, and set it in the `Makefile`, but that wouldn’t tell the tools “automatically assume Linux and don‘t fail because macOS doesn’t have that subpackage”.

That said, “this is unreasonable, just don’t do that — either configure your IDE to assume Linux somehow, possibly extending the IDE — or have the IDE connect to a Linux VM using existing remoting features” is a perfectly fine response to this RFC PR.

> I do have a vague concern that this will allow Libpod to be leaked into the build on Mac/Windows … though we probably still don't compile; there are no _other.go files that define the APIs that are compile-tagged out, after all.

Yes: without a `libpod.Container` definition most consumers clearly fail. We _might_ end up with some ~independent cross-platform utilities in `libpod/$feature.go`, perhaps even included in remote builds, when those utilities would be better somewhere in `pkg/$feature`, without anyone noticing. As long as we don’t have a stable API commitment, I think we can clean such things up any time.
I think I'm LGTM on this after thinking it over. Should be safe. Let's just avoid doing unsupported versions of the APIs unless we absolutely have to.
